text: The surgeon examines a large indirect right inguinal hernia, focusing on the right side of a bilateral inguinal hernia repair. The left side, which involved a smaller indirect inguinal hernia, is not shown in detail.

The surgeon addresses a large, chronically incarcerated indirect inguinal hernia with visible sliding characteristics and attachments to the cecal suspensory ligament. Scar tissue and small bowel mesentery are dissected to free the hernia, and a peritoneal flap is created to facilitate reduction.
The surgeon performs a lateral dissection on a large indirect hernia, preparing to transition to a medial dissection to facilitate the reduction of the hernia sac.

The surgeon dissects down into the space of Retzius, identifying Cooper's ligament and the pubic tubercle. The video then jumps forward, showing the completed dissection on the left side, with the bladder and surrounding tissues taken down medially.

The surgeon carefully dissects the chronically scarred hernia sac, avoiding contact with the bowel. The inferior epigastric vessels are visible, and the surgeon works close to them to navigate through dense scar tissue. The hernia sac is sufficiently loosened, allowing the assistant to reduce it.
The surgeon has reduced the hernia and is now taking down scar tissue and separating adhesions. The hernia sac is visibly releasing smoothly.

The surgeon applies tension with the left hand while using cautery to separate tissues, employing a technique of cauterizing, cutting, and pushing. The indirect hernia sac is finally brought down, with remaining scar tissue visible along the epigastric vessels.
The surgeon continues the dissection until the obturator canal is visible, indicating progress in the surgical field.

The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum from the retroperitoneum and gonadal vessels, ensuring enough space for the extra-large 3D max mesh to lay flat. Three 8-millimeter ports are being used during this procedure.

The surgeon rolls up the extra large mesh and places it inside a half-inch Penrose drain. The assistant inserts the drain into the abdominal cavity using a Kelly clamp, allowing the surgeon to pull the mesh through. A small slit is made to easily extract the mesh from the Penrose drain, which is removed at the end of the procedure.

The surgeon prepares to tack the large mesh into place on the left side, noting the smaller size of the hernia compared to the right side. The mesh is positioned and additional stitches are placed, particularly on the right side, to secure it over the indirect hernia defect.

The surgeon places an eighteen gauge angiocath inside the pocket to remove CO₂ from the sac, ensuring the mesh lays flat after closing the peritoneal flap. However, the right-sided mesh is observed folding up, prompting corrective action.
The surgeon closes the left side peritoneum and proceeds to close the right side, using sutures to secure the tissue layers.

The surgeon inspects the left side, noting the decompressed flap due to the angiocath, ensuring the mesh lays flat. On the right side, an indirect hernia sac is addressed by imbricating it with a vicryl suture to prevent bowel herniation. The surgeon observes the lateral aspect of the right-sided extra large mesh folding up. To correct this, a small incision is made on the flap to access and cauterize a band of lateral attachment, allowing the mesh to lay flat.
